coredata.dc:title: Machine learning assists the sensor array constructed by the tri-emission carbon dots to detect multiple metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yaoyao
Zhu, Peide
Xu, Quan
Wang, Juncheng
coredata.dc:description: Metal ions are related to the health of daily life, which urges to be detected and identified by efficient sensors. We first synthesize tri-emission carbon dots for the functional determination of six metal ions and obtain the multi-dimension data to construct the fluorescent sensor array in the work. Inspired by the data-driven method, machine learning is employed in a sensor array to detect and analyze the six metal ions. The ion classification and concentration models are constructed to identify metal ion types and corresponding concentrations. The classification model could correctly discriminate six metal ions, reaching 99.5 % accuracy and the concentration model exhibits an excellent performance range from 30 to 330 μM, with a mean concentration difference value of 0.12 μM and R2 value of 0.9845 for the unknown sample. Furthermore, six metal ions in tap water also can be distinguished with 96 % accuracy and the recovery rate is between 101.89 % and 106.37 %. This work shows that the machine learning combined sensor array has significant advantages in detecting multiple metal ions and provides a novel thought for analyzing sensing data, accelerating the development of data-driven strategies in the sensing field. © 2001 Elsevier Science. All rights reserved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Machine learning
Data-driven method
Carbon dots
Sensor array
Multiple ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of multiple metals ions with T-CDs sensor array
Construction machine learning model based on the T-CDs sensor array
Detection of metal ions in aqueous solution
Selection and optimization of the ion classification and concentration model
Sensing metal ions by machine learning models
Sensing metal ions in real samples by machine learning models

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: J. C. Wang acknowledges the support from the Beijing National Science Foundation (No. L222109) and Military Health Care Project (No. 22BJZ22), Quan Xu acknowledges the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52211530034) and the Beijing National Science Foundation (No. 3222018).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of T-CDs (inset: HRTEM of T-CDs). (b) The size distribution of 200 particles. (c) AFM of T-CDs (inset: the diagram of height on white line). (d) The XRD of T-CDs.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of T-CDs under excitation wavelength ranging from 290 to 440 nm. (b) FT-IR spectrum of T-CDs. (c) The full XPS spectrum of T-CDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e), O 1 s (f).

The relationship between the fluorescence intensity changes and the concentration of six ions ((a): Cd2+, (b)Co2+, (c): Cu2+, (d): Fe3+, (e): Mn2+, (f): Zn2+) in the range of 0–330 μM. (insert: the linear relationship between the low concentration and the intensity changes). I370 and I490 represent the fluorescence intensity at 370 nm and 490 nm, respectively.

(a) The heat map of T-CDs responding signals under the concentration of 120 μM metal ions. (b) Three-dimensional principal component analysis (PCA) plot of the fluorescent sensor array for all sense data. (c) The flow diagram of machine learning application.

(a) Feature importance of MLP ion classification model analyzed by SHAP method. (b) The confusion matrix heat map of the predicted and true categories on the test dataset. (c) The fitting linear curve on predicted and true categories. (d) The histogram of the grey relational coefficient of the selected feature to concentration relationships. Performance of ion concentration models on the validated dataset. (e) The difference between the true and predicted concentrations on every single ion kind. (f) The fitting linear curve on predicted and true concentrations.

The performance of ion classification and concentration model on real sample. (a) The confusion matrix of the ion classification model for six metal ions compares the true and predicted categories. (b) The fitting linear curve between the predicted and true types on the actual sample. (c) The capacity of every kind of ion concentration model to predict unknown samples is evaluated by R2 and MAE metrics. (d) The fitting linear curve between predicted concentration and true concentration on all real samples.
The schematic diagram of machine learning applied in the metal ions detection.
The LOD of six ions under the T-CDs.

head.ce:title: Machine learning assists the sensor array constructed by the tri-emission carbon dots to detect multiple metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collegel of Information Science and Engineering/College of Artificial Intelligence, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collegel of Information Science and Engineering/College of Artificial Intelligence

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collegel of Information Science and Engineering/College of Artificial Intelligence, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Stomatology, First Medical Center, Chinese PLA General Hospital, Beijing 100853, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Stomatology, First Medical Center, Chinese PLA General Hospital, Beijing 100853, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China (Q. Xu). Institute of Stomatology, First Medical Center, Chinese PLA General Hospital, Beijing 100853, China (JC. Wang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We synthesized tri-emission carbon dots to construct a high-dimensional sensor array for multiple metal ion detection. The machine learning based on the sensor array can detect six metal ions and discern the corresponding concentration. For the unknown sample in deionized water, the model can completely distinguish different ion types and provide concentration with a 0.12 μM average error. Furthermore, the actual environmental sample also tests with 96% accuracy and the recovery rate is between 101.89 % and 106.37 %.

Metal ions are related to the health of daily life, which urges to be detected and identified by efficient sensors. We first synthesize tri-emission carbon dots for the functional determination of six metal ions and obtain the multi-dimension data to construct the fluorescent sensor array in the work. Inspired by the data-driven method, machine learning is employed in a sensor array to detect and analyze the six metal ions. The ion classification and concentration models are constructed to identify metal ion types and corresponding concentrations. The classification model could correctly discriminate six metal ions, reaching 99.5 % accuracy and the concentration model exhibits an excellent performance range from 30 to 330 μM, with a mean concentration difference value of 0.12 μM and R2 value of 0.9845 for the unknown sample. Furthermore, six metal ions in tap water also can be distinguished with 96 % accuracy and the recovery rate is between 101.89 % and 106.37 %. This work shows that the machine learning combined sensor array has significant advantages in detecting multiple metal ions and provides a novel thought for analyzing sensing data, accelerating the development of data-driven strategies in the sensing field. © 2001 Elsevier Science. All rights reserved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of industrial production will bring a variety of environmental pollution, and the impact of heavy metal is of particular concern [1–3]. With daily production and life, heavy metals are discharged into the water body, affecting the quality of the water environment and threatening a variety of biological and even human health [4]. Therefore, detecting heavy metal ions in the water is essential to monitoring environmental health and ensuring food safety [5]. The detection and quantification of heavy metals include the electrochemical method [6–8], spectrometric method [9–11], plasma mass spectrometry [12–14], and so on [15]. Considering the cost, detection convenience, sensitivity, and accuracy requirements, the spectrometric method is more conducive to the analysis of metal ions [16]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, which are widely considered as spectroscopic analysis due to their unique low toxicity, high fluorescence, and chemical stability [17–19]. Researchers have synthesized various fluorescent CDs, which can detect different metal ions with high precision and selectivity. For example, Sun et al. [20] utilized L-cysteine to synthesize the blue CDs which were applied to detect Co2+ whose detect limit is 0.026 μM. Xu et al. [21] prepare blue carbon dots whose fluorescence is sensitive to Fe3+, and the detection limit is 0.057 μM.

Machine learning (ML) belongs to artificial intelligence and develops fast under the explosive data increase and powerful computational ability [22–24]. It can handle complex and enormous information to find an inner discipline, even accurately predict unknown events, whose ability often exceeds the human being [25,26] and helps to enlarge its application field such as risk management [27–29], drug development [30–32], speech recognition [33], intelligent manufacturing [34], and so on. With the ML technology maturing and achieving tremendous success, more and more researchers are acceding the stream of using new approaches. Tang and co-workers apply ML to instruct the process of CDs’ synthesis and improve the quantum yield [35]. Hu et al. [36] used principal component analysis (PCA) to discriminate 11 metal ions and 7 thiols by different fluorescence response patterns. Moreover, the negatively charged glycosaminoglycans and tetracycline also adopt similar methods to detect the kinds and even calculate the concentration [37,38]. The combination of the ML and CDs is extensive which can enhance the relevant performance, shorten the trial experiment, and create novel analysis methods, etc.

In this study, we synthesized tri-emission carbon dots (T-CDs) to construct a high-dimensional sensor array for multiple metal ion detection, as shown in Scheme 1 . Due to the sensitivity of T-CDs to metal ions, the sensor incorporating fluorescent intensity variation and ratio shows different responses. Machine learning is applied to the sensor array, building the ion classification and concentration models were used to detect and quantify ion types and concentration, respectively. Next, the nested cross-validation is used in the processing of model selection to choose the best-performing models. These models show great success in sensing the metal ions which can correctly discriminate the different ion kinds and predict specific concentration. Furthermore, the models are tested on tap water, whose result also exhibits excellent performance on the unknown sample, demonstrating the universality and applicability of models. Overall, the application of ML in sensing ions is feasible and outstanding, which demonstrates the association of machine learning and CDs’ application and provides a new approach for breaking new ground in detecting other substances in the future.

β-Cyclodextrin, o-phenylenediamine, Cd (NO3)2, CuCl2, FeCl3, MnCl2, ZnCl2, CoCl2 AlCl3 BaCl2, CaCl2, CrCl3, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, SnCl2, PbCl2, NaNO3, and MgSO4 were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Urea and glucose came from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), acetic acid can be directly used without further purification. Deionized water produced by BK-10B from Dongguanshi Qianjing Environmental Equipment Co, Ltd.

The geometrical morphology of the CDs was analyzed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on FEI Talos f200s under the 200 kV accelerating voltage. The microstructure on the surface is tested through the atom force microscope (AFM) (Bruker Scan-Dimension-Icon System). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured by Bruker D8 ADVANCE. The optical properties, including photoluminescence, optical absorption, and life curves utilize the instrument of fluorescence spectrometer (FS5) from Techcomp (China Ltd). The functional groups and chemical structure are examined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), whose apparatus are Nicolet iS50 and AXIS-Ultra DLD, respectively.

T-CDs were prepared and some procedures were based on the previous report [18]. 1.2 g β-Cyclodextrin and 0.3 g o-phenylenediamine were added into the DMF solution (7 mL). Then 1 mL acetic acid (1 %) was added to the mixture and stirred well. Next, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon reactor and kept at 200 °C for 10 h in an air oven. T-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment while the solution cooled to ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of multiple metals ions with T-CDs sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: T-CDs were synthesized as sensor elements to detect the metal ions. The six different metal ions (Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+) at a concentration of 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 210, 240, 270, 300, and 330 μM were added into T-CDs sensor.

After mixing, the fluorescence emission was measured and emission peak intensity was recorded. The emission spectrum can observe a strong peak at 370 nm and a weak peak at 490 nm while the excitation wavelength at 340 nm and own single peak at 560 nm while the excitation wavelength at 420 nm (Fig. S1). All experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction machine learning model based on the T-CDs sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The emission peak at 370 and 490 nm were marked as F1 and F2 and the peak at 560 nm was marked as F3. F0 and F represented the intensity of emission peaks before and after adding metal ions, respectively. Three fluorescent peak intensity variation (ΔF = (F0-F)/F0) and three intensity ratios (R1 = F1/F2, R2 = F1/F3, R3 = F2/F3) were calculated. The sensor array has six channels consisting of fluorescent intensity variation and ratios, which can provide sufficient data for machine learning. So the models (ion classification model and ion concentration model) are built for specific detection.

The six-channel data act as the input feature, and the ions kinds act as the output feature for the classification model. The models could distinguish the ion kinds according to the fluorescent response between the T-CDs and metal ions. Subsequently, the ion concentration model is trained for every metal ion while the input feature remains unchanged and the output is numerical concentration. The concentration model aims to provide and predict the detailed number of specific metal ions. Integrating the classification models and following concentration models can detect and quantify the metal ion, providing a novel method in the detection field.

The experiment was conducted in tap water further to inspect the universality of the machine learning model. Six metal ions (Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+) dissolve in tap water and dilute to the preset concentration (30, 50, 100, 200, and 300 μM). The emission spectrum is measured and recorded at excitation wavelengths of 340 and 420 nm after mixing the T-CDs and metal ion solution. Three copies of metal ion samples in every concentration are prepared, and the experiment repeats three times in parallel to decrease the accidental error.

The geometrical morphology of T-CDs was observed by TEM and AFM, as shown in Fig. 1 . T-CDs have spherical and well-dispersed particles in the water and their uniform size ranges from 4 ∼ 8 nm according to TEM (Fig. 1a). The size of T-CDs exhibits a normal distribution with an average size of 6.15 nm based on counting 200 particles (Fig. 1b).

The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image can observe a clear crystal lattice with a distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice facet of graphite carbon, revealing T-CDs own the graphite-like structure [39,40]. AFM investigates T-CDs' height, as shown in Fig. 1c, which is distributed evenly with a mean thickness of 3.5 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) of tri-emission CDs has a sharp peak near 18°, exhibiting excellent crystallinity, which is related to the small size of CDs [41,42]. The above figure indicates that T-CDs have an elliptical structure and good crystallinity, which fits well with the definition of CDs.

The fluorescence emission spectra of T-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 a, which has three major emission peaks corresponding to 370, 490, and 560 nm. Additionally, the emission peaks are gradually red-shifted as the excitation wavelength increases. The pH and time stability of T-CDs were also tested for further application. The intensity of three emission peaks remained unchanged in varied pH ranging from 3 to 12 in Fig. S2, presenting the broad application of T-CDs in extreme conditions. After exposing 1 h in the air, the photoluminescence of the T-CDs has no change, as shown in Fig. S3. T-CDs own five representative absorption peaks in FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2b). The broad absorption at 3300 cm−1 is attributed to the N-H and O-H stretching vibration, demonstrating the CDs are rich in hydrophilic groups, causing high solubility in water. The peak at 2930 cm−1 is related to the C-H stretching vibration. The band at 1650 cm−1 corresponded to the C = O/C = C structure on the surface of CDs [43]. In addition, the absorption peak at 1410 and 1000 cm−1 came from the C-N and C-O [44,45]. Next, the XPS also demonstrated the chemical bond and functional group. The full XPS spectrum of T-CDs in Fig. 2c contains C, N, and O elements whose atomic ratios are 50.60 %, 3.00 %, and 46.40 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s, four prominent peaks at 284.5 eV,285.1 eV, 286.4 eV, 288.3 eV are assigned to the C = C/C-C, C-N, C-O, and C = N/C = O, respectively [40,46,47]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) is resolved into pyridinic N (399.07 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.20 eV) [48]. The O1s spectrum is fitted into two dominant peaks at 532.3 eV and 533.2 eV, attributing to the C = O and C-O [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Owning to the excellent optical properties of T-CDs, it can construct the multi-dimension sensor and be used as the metal probe. As shown in Fig. S4, the three emission peaks have different responses for the various metal ions addition resulting in unique fluorescence spectra. With the ion concentration increase, the intensity of fluorescence at 370, 490, and 560 nm was gradually quenched, while the peak at 370 nm dramatically decreased. Fig. 3 shows the linear relationship between the concentration of ions and the variation of fluorescence intensity (F370, F490) and the ratio of (I370/I490). The R2 of all ions both above 0.98, and the limit of detection (LOD) is less than 10 μM. (LOD = 3 ơ/s, ơ represents the standard deviation of three blank samples and s is the slope of the linear relationship). The detailed information of all ions is concluded in Table 1 .

After adding six metals, the three peaks of T-CDs both exhibit a downward trend and the coexistence also influences the fluorescence intensity change. Mn2+ and Zn2+ were randomly selected to mix with concentrations 0 μM /50 μM, 25 μM /25 μM, and 50 μM /0 μM. The fluorescence intensity drops dramatically while adding binary mixtures, even more than two individual metal ions (Fig. S5), which indicates that Mn2+ and Zn2+ can synergistically quench T-CDs. The binary metals are mixed according to the concentration ratio 0/100, 10/90, 20/80, 30/70, 40/60, 50/50, 60/40, 70/30, 80/20, 90/10, and 100/0, maintaining total concentration at 50 μM, and measure the intensity at 370 nm. As depicted in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity changes and the concentration ratio have a nonlinear relationship, indicating that T-CDs are suitable for single ions detection.

Selectivity is a considerable impactor for CDs to detect ions. To further verify the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs for six metal ions, we test the metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Sn2+, Pb2+), anions (NO3 –, SO4 2-) and organic small molecule (glucose, urea). As portrayed in Fig. S7, these interferences show no fluorescence change, indicating that they have little influence on T-CDs.

To further explore the mechanism of detection, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence lifetime are measured in Fig. S8. Next, the time-dependent single-photon counting spectra of the T-CDs are recorded, as displayed in Fig. S9 and Table S1. For Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Mn2+, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of T-CDs notably enhanced with increasing ion concentration and the lifetime has little change after adding ions, forming a nonfluorescent ground-state complex between T-CDs and metal ions, belonging to static quench [49]. For Co2+ and Zn2+, the absorption spectrum has no change and lifetime both decrease, illustrating charge transfer or energy transfer on the process of T-CDs returns to ground state, which belongs to dynamic quench [49].

The lifetime of T-CDs presents exponential decay whose lifetime is 1.78 and 4.71 ns under the 340 and 420 nm excitation, respectively. While the T-CDs added the metal ions, the fluorescence lifetime exhibited a noticeable difference. The above phenomena manifest the mechanism between T-CDs and metal ions belonging to the dynamic quenching [41,44].

T-CDs were considered the sensor element to build the multi-channel sensor array. Compared with past surveys, the fluorescent intensity ratio was added to the sense signal to enhance the effect of discriminating metal ions. As illustrated in Fig. S10, while the ΔF + R as input data, the model’s performance was 97 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.87 %. In contrast, the model’s performance using ΔF data as input data decreased by 95 %, with an RSD of 0.32 %. The fluorescent intensity ratio data are important for enhancing detection accuracy which is consistent with previous studies [37,38,43].

In this work, the high-dimension response signal contains the fluorescent intensity variation (ΔF1, ΔF2, ΔF3) and fluorescent intensity (R1, R2, R3) as displayed in Fig. 4 a. In addition, due to the kinds and concentration of metal ions, the visualization by heat map gets tedious and interminable. To effectively observe the spatial distribution of all sense data, the principal component analysis (PCA) is adopted and the dimension of data reduces the three. As presented in Fig. 4b, the data in three dimensions for different species of metal ions do not overlap and cross, indicating that the data array facilitates the discrimination of different kinds of metal ions, which was easier to distinguish in the original high-dimensional data. Therefore, utilizing high-dimensional datasets for the model is feasible to quantify various metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection and optimization of the ion classification and concentration model
ce:section.ce:para: The machine learning model consists of the ion classification and ion concentration model, whose flow diagram is displayed in Fig. 4c. The sensor array data for unknown samples firstly were discriminated kinds by the classification model. Subsequently, the corresponding concentration model outputs the concentration value. Based on the “no free lunch” theory, no generalized learning algorithm can perform well in all kinds of tasks, and specific problems need to be explicitly analyzed. To find out the suitable algorithm for the ion classification model, the Naïve Bayes (NB), random forest (RF), multilayer perceptron (MLP), and XGBoost (XGB) were considered as the candidates. These models represent four typical ML algorithms and have been successfully used in the materials field [50]. Evaluation metrics containing F1 score, precision, and recall were selected to choose the best multi-classification metal ions model algorithm. In addition, the model candidates were optimized to compare the performance by the five-fold nested cross-validation. The nested cross-validation (Fig. S11) consists of inner cross-validation and outer cross-validation, while the inner loop searches for the best model hyperparameters and the outer loop evaluates the model’s performance on the unbeknown data. Further, all the dataset is shuffled and randomly assigned to training and test sets, so the method can effectively avoid overfitting and underfitting. As shown in Fig. S12 and Table S2, the MLP exhibits the best performance from boxplots of the four candidate models, indicating the MLP generates the best accordance with the classification outcomes and excellent generalization ability on unseen datasets.

The selection of the ion concentration model is similar to the ion classification model. The regressor algorithm of Ridge (RG), random forest (RF), multilayer perceptron (MLP), and XGBoost (XGB) are optimized by nested cross-validation. The performance metrics, including coefficient of determination (R2), mean squared error (MSE) and mean absolute error (MAE) are regarded as the evaluation criteria. The detailed metrics results of four models are compared in Fig. S13 and Table S4, and XGB is selected as the best model due to the higher R2 and lower MSE and MAE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing metal ions by machine learning models
ce:section.ce:para: Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) based on the coalitional game theory harmonize the explanatory forecasting framework of the six existing additive feature attribution methods and define new additive feature importance measures, which conform more to human intuition [51].

MLP, a classical black box model, uses the SHAP to explain the feature importance, as displayed in Fig. 5 a. The fluorescent peak intensity variation at 560 nm emission wavelength (ΔF3) plays the most crucial character in distinguishing the metal ions and the peak intensity ratio (R3, R1) is subsequent, which is consistent with the previous data analysis and proves the importance and necessity of R's existence in the dataset. The MLP model is tested on the new dataset from the unknown sample. As indicated from the confusion matrix heat map (Fig. 5b), all 39 samples can be completely and correctly separated from each other, resulting in 100 % accuracy for single metal ions (Table S3). Additionally, the corresponding fitting curves in Fig. 5c show the excellent linear relationship between the real and predicted categories whose R2 is 1, confirming the accuracy and validity of MLP.

The relationship between the input feature and concentration is calculated by the grey relation analysis (GRA), which quantifies the correlation degree and determines the extent to which each factor affects the concentration [52]. From the GRA result (Fig. 5d), the fluorescent intensity variation is more relevant than the intensity ratio in general, which is consistent with the fluorescence ratio method. Notably, the emission wavelength at 490 nm is most directly related to the concentration. The new dataset from the unknown sample is applied to test the performance. The difference value is presented in Fig. 5e and Table S5, whose average difference value is 0.12 μM, exhibiting excellent prediction ability. The relationship between the actual and predicted concentration is linear, as shown in Fig. 5f, and fitted as a linear function whose R2 is 0.9884.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing metal ions in real samples by machine learning models
ce:section.ce:para: The performance is tested on actual samples to verify further the availability and universality of the ion classification and concentration model. All 90 real samples at concentrations of 30, 50, 100, 200, and 300 μM were prepared in tap water. The corresponding fluorescence spectrum mixing with T-CDs is recorded and relevant feature parameters as model input are calculated. As depicted in Fig. 6 a and Table S6, the sample was discriminated with 96 % accuracy through the ion classification model. Besides, the accuracy in the five categories is above 90 %, while the ions of Cd2+, Co2+, and Fe3+ can be completely correctly identified and the accuracy reaches 100 %. The relationship between the true and predicted categories is fitted by a linear function with 0.8766 R2, as shown in Fig. 6b.

Next, the concentration is predicted through the corresponding ion concentration model. As provided in Fig. 6c and Fig. S14, the concentration error ranges from 3.5 to 7, and the R2 of the linear fitting curve on six kinds of predicted concentration and true concentration is above 0.99 (Fig. S15). Moreover, the recovery rate is also calculated, and Table S7 presents the result in detail. The recovery rate on six kinds of ions is between 101.89 % and 106.37 %, with a mean value of 104.16 %. Furthermore, the fitting linear curve between all predicted and actual concentrations is depicted in Fig. 6d, whose R2 value is 0.9960. Above all, results illustrate the superior availability of our sensor arrays for discrimination of unknown samples, demonstrating machine learning has potential applications in sensing detection.

In summary, the data-driven method for quantitative metal ions in water was innovatively constructed by combining machine learning with a sensor array. The sensor array is based on the T-CDs to gain the high-dimension matrix data by simple means, while machine learning is good at handling and discovering the data’s inner discipline, resulting in forming a data-centered analytics approach. The method builds ion classification and concentration models to predict the category and corresponding concentration. The analysis of SHAP and GRA is adopted to explain the model and meet the logic discipline. Then, six metal ions were successfully identified with 100 % accuracy and at a concentration mean difference error as low as 0.12 μM, which proved that ML could detect multiple ions over a wide range of concentrations. Furthermore, the method has excellent robustness, stability, and anti-interference, exhibiting brilliant performance in the natural environment. Different concentrations of metal ions in actual tap water could be distinguished with 96 % accuracy, and the R2 value reached 0.996 between the predicted and true concentrations in all samples. Finally, this study demonstrates that machine learning possesses a promising potential for quickly and accurately detecting the multiple metal ions in the solution, and a data-driven model is a valuable tool in the data analysis method. So we believe machine learning can be more applied to accelerate technology innovation than traditional methods.

Yaoyao Tang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Peide Zhu: Investigation, Data curation. Quan Xu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Juncheng Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J. C. Wang acknowledges the support from the Beijing National Science Foundation (No. L222109) and Military Health Care Project (No. 22BJZ22), Quan Xu acknowledges the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52211530034) and the Beijing National Science Foundation (No. 3222018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110536.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Sci. Total Environ.

Anal. Chem.
